1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
7	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
8	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
9	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
10	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
12	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
13	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
14	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
15	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
16	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
17	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
18	of the trivial sheaf.
19	
20	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
21	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
22	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
23	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
24	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
25	
26	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
27	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
28	
29	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
30	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
31	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
32	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
33	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
34	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
35	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
36	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
37	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
38	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
39	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
40	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
41	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
42	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
43	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
44	
45	Stating the triple-overlap multiplicativity of the descent datum, however, first
46	requires a small piece of base-change machinery for the pullback functor on sheaves
47	of modules: the three transition isomorphisms attached to a triple of charts live a
48	priori on three different overlaps, and to compare their composite they must be
49	transported to a single common overlap. The next two blocks supply exactly this
50	transport -- the pseudofunctorial comparison of iterated pullbacks
51	(\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}) and its packaging along the triple-overlap data of a
52	scheme glue datum (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) -- after which the
53	cocycle condition can be expressed cleanly.
54	
55	\begin{definition}[Comparison of iterated pullbacks of sheaves of modules]
56	  \label{def:modules_pullbackComp}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}
58	  \mathlibok
59	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
60	  Let \(a : X \to Y\) and \(b : Y \to Z\) be morphisms of schemes. For a sheaf of
61	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\), pulling back first along \(b\) and then
62	  along \(a\) agrees with pulling back along the composite \(b \circ a\): there is a
63	  natural isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
64	  \[
65	    a^{*}\bigl(b^{*}\mathcal{M}\bigr)
66	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
67	    (b \circ a)^{*}\mathcal{M},
68	  \]
69	  natural in \(\mathcal{M}\), expressing that \(\mathcal{M} \mapsto \mathcal{M}\)'s
70	  pullback is a pseudofunctor on schemes. These comparison isomorphisms satisfy the
71	  pentagon (associativity) coherence for a triple of composable morphisms. This is the
72	  pullback half of the pseudofunctoriality of sheaves of modules and is supplied by
73	  Mathlib.
74	\end{definition}
75	
76	\begin{lemma}\leanok
77	[Base-change transport of a transition isomorphism to a triple overlap]
78	  \label{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
79	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackBaseChangeTransport}
80	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
81	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) with charts
82	  \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), overlap immersions \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow
83	  U_i\), transitions \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\), and triple-overlap
84	  data \(t'_{ijk}\) with its compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\). For an index
85	  triple \((i,j,k)\) write
86	  \[
87	    V_{ijk} \;=\; V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}
88	  \]
89	  for the common triple overlap, with the two projections
90	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) and \(p^{ik}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ik}\), and
91	  let \(t'_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{jki}\) be the glue datum's triple
92	  transition, satisfying
93	  \[
94	    t'_{ijk} \,\circ\, p^{jk}_{jki} \;=\; p^{ij}_{ijk} \,\circ\, t_{ij}
95	    \qquad (t_{\mathrm{fac}}).
96	  \]
97	  Given per-chart sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and a transition isomorphism
98	  \[
99	    g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
100	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
101	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
102	  \]
103	  on the overlap \(V_{ij}\) (as in \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}), the
104	  \emph{base-change transport} of \(g_{ij}\) to the triple overlap \(V_{ijk}\) is the
105	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{ijk}}\)-modules
106	  \[
107	    \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
108	      \;:\;
109	    (f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
110	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
111	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
112	  \]
113	  obtained by pulling \(g_{ij}\) back along \(p^{ij}_{ijk}\) and reassociating the
114	  iterated pullbacks via \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. With the analogous transports
115	  \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) (the first taken along
116	  \(t'_{ijk}\), so that \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identifies its source and target sheaves
117	  with those of \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\)), all three
118	  transported isomorphisms become endomorphism-composable: \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\)
119	  and \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) land on a common sheaf, and their composite is
120	  comparable to \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) on \(V_{ijk}\).
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
125	  The pullback functor on sheaves of modules is a pseudofunctor: for composable
126	  scheme morphisms it carries the comparison isomorphisms of
127	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}, natural in the sheaf and coherent for triple
128	  composites. Pulling the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\) back along the projection
129	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) yields an isomorphism between
130	  \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*} f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}(t_{ij} \circ
131	  f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\); applying \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} to each side
132	  reassociates these into the single-pullback form
133	  \((f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and
134	  \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\), giving
135	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\). For the \((j,k)\)-transition the same construction is
136	  applied along the triple transition \(t'_{ijk}\) rather than a bare projection; the
137	  glue datum's compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\),
138	  \(t'_{ijk} \circ p^{jk}_{jki} = p^{ij}_{ijk} \circ t_{ij}\), guarantees that the
139	  source of \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) coincides with the target of
140	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) (both are the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}_j\) along the same
141	  morphism \(V_{ijk} \to U_j\)), so the two are composable as endomorphism-style
142	  isomorphisms over \(V_{ijk}\). The reassociations introduced by
143	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} are coherent (they satisfy the pentagon identity), so
144	  the composite \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and the third
145	  transport \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are isomorphisms between the same pair of
146	  sheaves on \(V_{ijk}\), and may be compared directly. This is the module-level
147	  analogue of the scheme-level triangle \(t'_{ijk}\)/\(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) and is the
148	  shape in which the multiplicative cocycle condition is stated below.
149	\end{proof}
150	
151	\begin{lemma}[Triple-overlap endpoint bridges]
152	  \label{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
153	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_src,
154	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_mid,
155	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_tgt}
156	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
157	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) and fix
158	  indices \(i,j,k\) with triple overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\).
159	  The three transports of \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} have
160	  endpoints pinned down by three scheme-morphism equalities on \(V_{ijk}\), each a
161	  pure consequence of the glue datum's structural identities:
162	  \begin{itemize}
163	    \item (\emph{src}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ f_{ij} = \mathrm{snd} \circ f_{ik}\)
164	      -- the defining pullback condition of \(V_{ijk}\);
165	    \item (\emph{mid}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})
166	      = (t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ f_{jk}\) -- the pullback condition
167	      composed with the glue datum's \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identity;
168	    \item (\emph{tgt}) \((t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ (t_{jk} \circ f_{kj})
169	      = \mathrm{snd} \circ (t_{ik} \circ f_{ki})\) -- the cocycle core, from the
170	      \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) associativity, the reversed pullback condition, the
171	      \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\) collapse and the glue datum's cocycle identity.
172	  \end{itemize}
173	  These three equalities align the source, middle, and target sheaves of the three
174	  transports so that the cocycle equation (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}
175	  typechecks.
176	\end{lemma}
177	\begin{proof}
178	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
179	  Each equality is an identity of morphisms in the category of schemes, proved by
180	  rewriting with the glue datum's axioms: \emph{src} is literally
181	  \(\mathrm{pullback.condition}\); \emph{mid} adds the \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)
182	  factorisation \(D.t_{\mathrm{fac}}\,i\,j\,k\); \emph{tgt} chains
183	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at \((j,k,i)\), the reversed pullback
184	  condition, an auxiliary collapse combining \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at
185	  \((k,i,j)\) with \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\), and finally the cocycle identity
186	  \(D.\mathrm{cocycle}\,i\,j\,k\). No sheaf-of-modules data enters; these are
187	  bookkeeping equalities of the underlying scheme cover.
188	\end{proof}
189	
190	\begin{definition}
191	
192	\leanok
193	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
194	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
195	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
196	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:modules_pullbackComp,
197	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
198	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
199	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
200	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
201	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
202	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
203	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
204	  charts. Suppose given:
205	  \begin{itemize}
206	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
207	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
208	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
209	      \[
210	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
211	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
212	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
213	      \]
214	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
215	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
216	  \end{itemize}
217	  \emph{subject to the following module cocycle conditions}, compatible with the
218	  scheme-level cocycle of \(D\) (its triple transition data \(t_{ij}\) and the
219	  overlap immersions \(f_{ij}\)), which are required as hypotheses on the family
220	  \((g_{ij})\):
221	  \begin{enumerate}
222	    \item[(C1)] \emph{self-identity:} the self-transition is the identity,
223	      \(g_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\) on \(f_{ii}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) (with
224	      \(V_{ii}\) and \(t_{ii}\) the diagonal data of \(D\));
225	    \item[(C2)] \emph{triple-overlap multiplicativity:} over each common triple
226	      overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\) the base-change transports
227	      (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the three transition
228	      isomorphisms satisfy
229	      \[
230	        \widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
231	          \;=\; \widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j},
232	      \]
233	      where \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\), \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\),
234	      \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are the transports of \(g_{ij}\), \(g_{jk}\),
235	      \(g_{ik}\) to \(V_{ijk}\) through the scheme-level triple transition
236	      \(t'_{ijk}\) and its compatibility \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\), reassociated by the
237	      pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} so that all three land on
238	      the same sheaf -- the multiplicative, \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle
239	      condition.
240	  \end{enumerate}
241	  Conditions (C1) and (C2) are exactly the descent datum for the Zariski cover
242	  \(\{\iota_i\}\); it is subject to them that the gluing below is well-defined and
243	  the glued sheaf is characterised up to unique isomorphism. Out of this data the
244	  construction produces:
245	  \begin{enumerate}
246	    \item a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\); and
247	    \item for each \(i \in J\) a \emph{restriction isomorphism}
248	      \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
249	      identifying the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}\) along \(\iota_i\) with the
250	      \(i\)-th chart sheaf, the \(\rho_i\) being compatible with the transition
251	      isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps.
252	  \end{enumerate}
253	  The pair \((\mathcal{M}, (\rho_i)_i)\) is characterised up to unique
254	  isomorphism by the restriction property: \(\mathcal{M}\) is the unique sheaf of
255	  modules on \(X\) (up to canonical isomorphism) restricting to each
256	  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) compatibly with \((g_{ij})\). The same primitive descends
257	  morphisms: given two such glued data \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and
258	  \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\), and
259	  a family of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms
260	  \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) that commute with the transition
261	  isomorphisms on every overlap, there is a unique morphism
262	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) with
263	  \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\)
264	  for every \(i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the cover.
265	
266	  \emph{Construction.} A sheaf of modules on \(X\) is equivalent to a compatible
267	  family of sheaves on the members of an open cover of \(X\) together with
268	  identifications on the overlaps -- effective descent for the Zariski cover
269	  \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\). The charts \(U_i\), being the images of
270	  the glue datum's objects, form such a cover, with intersections
271	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) identified with the overlaps \(V_{ij}\) via the structural data
272	  of \(D\). Pulling the sections of an abstract \(\mathcal{M}\) back to each chart
273	  must reproduce \(\mathcal{M}_i\); the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\),
274	  satisfying the cocycle conditions, are exactly the descent datum that makes the
275	  family \((\mathcal{M}_i)\) glue, so the descent produces \(\mathcal{M}\) with
276	  the asserted restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\). The morphism statement is the
277	  descent of morphisms for the same cover: a morphism on \(X\) is a family of
278	  morphisms on the charts agreeing on overlaps, and the compatibility of the
279	  \(\phi_i\) with \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\) is precisely the agreement condition,
280	  giving the unique glued \(\phi\). This specialises the general descent of sheaves
281	  of modules to the concrete open cover supplied by a scheme glue datum,
282	  and is the form in which the Grassmannian's tautological bundle and quotient are
283	  built.
284	
285	  \emph{Realisation (construction path).} Concretely, instantiate \(D\) as the
286	  Grassmannian glue datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with glued scheme
287	  \(X = \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) and its canonical open cover by the charts
288	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\). The glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is built from
289	  the per-chart sheaves \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and the overlap transition isomorphisms
290	  \(g_{ij}\) satisfying the module cocycle conditions, and is pinned down by the
291	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim}
292	  \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\). For an open immersion
293	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\), the pullback \(\iota_i^{*}\) restricts sheaves
294	  of modules on \(X\) to sheaves of modules on \(U_i\). The transition isomorphisms
295	  \(g_{ij}\), forming a cocycle for this cover, constitute a descent datum; gluing the
296	  local sections yields \(\mathcal{M}\) together with the restriction isomorphisms
297	  \(\rho_i\), where both the assembly and the characterisation up to unique isomorphism
298	  rest on the locality of sections of a sheaf -- a compatible family of sections over
299	  the cover, agreeing on the overlaps, extends to a unique global section.
300	
301	  The morphism-descent half is the same locality argument applied to homomorphisms:
302	  a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) commuting with the transition
303	  isomorphisms over every overlap is a compatible family of local morphisms, and the
304	  uniqueness of gluing of sections promotes it to a unique global
305	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) restricting
306	  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\).
307	\end{definition}
308	
309	\subsection{Pullback of free sheaves along an arbitrary morphism}
310	\label{sec:grquot_pullback_free}
311	
312	The functor of points (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}) acts on morphisms by pullback, so
313	it needs the pullback of the trivial bundle to be again trivial --
314	\(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I} \cong \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}\) -- for an
315	\emph{arbitrary} scheme morphism \(\varphi\). Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison applies
316	only when the underlying site functor \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is \emph{final}; the
317	following three blocks remove that restriction for every morphism, and the resulting
318	pullback machinery preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness.
319	
320	\begin{lemma}[The opens-pullback functor is final]
321	  \label{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
322	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.opensMap_final}
323	  For any morphism of schemes \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the inverse-image functor on opens
324	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi : \mathrm{Opens}(T) \to \mathrm{Opens}(T')\),
325	  \(U \mapsto \varphi^{-1}U\), is \emph{final}.
326	\end{lemma}
327	
328	\begin{proof}
329	  Finality means that for every open \(V \subseteq T'\) the structured-arrow category of
330	  opens \(U \subseteq T\) with \(V \le \varphi^{-1}U\) is connected. That category is
331	  nonempty and has the terminal object \(U = \top\) -- one always has
332	  \(V \le \varphi^{-1}\top = T'\), and every object admits a unique arrow to \(\top\) --
333	  and a category with a terminal object is connected. This is the general
334	  ``pullback of free is free'' unlock, reusable beyond this chapter.
335	\end{proof}
336	
337	\begin{lemma}[Pullback of a free sheaf is free]
338	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
339	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso}
340	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
341	  For any scheme morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\) and index type \(I\), there is a canonical
342	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{T'}\)-modules
343	  \[
344	    \varphi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I}\bigr)
345	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
346	    \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}.
347	  \]
348	\end{lemma}
349	
350	\begin{proof}
351	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
352	  Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison produces this isomorphism whenever the site functor
353	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is final; that finality is \cref{lem:gr_opensMap_final},
354	  which holds for every morphism, so the comparison applies unconditionally.
355	\end{proof}
356	
357	\begin{lemma}[Pullback preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness]
358	  \label{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
359	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
360	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
361	  Let \(\varphi : T' \to T\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) a sheaf of
362	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules locally free of rank \(d\)
363	  (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}). Then the pullback \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is
364	  locally free of rank \(d\) on \(T'\).
365	\end{lemma}
366	
367	\begin{proof}
368	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
369	  Choose an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(T\) trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\),
370	  \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\). The preimages
371	  \(\{\varphi^{-1}U_i\}\) cover \(T'\). On each member the factorisation
372	  \(\varphi \circ \iota_{\varphi^{-1}U_i} = (\varphi|_{U_i}) \circ \iota_{U_i}\) of the
373	  restricted morphism, together with the pseudofunctor comparison of iterated pullbacks
374	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}), identifies
375	  \((\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M})|_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}\) with
376	  \((\varphi|_{U_i})^{*}\mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\); the latter is
377	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}^{\,d}\) by \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. Hence
378	  \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is locally free of rank \(d\).
379	\end{proof}
380	
381	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
382	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
383	
384	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
385	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
386	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
387	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
388	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
389	
390	\begin{definition}\leanok
391	[Global section of the unit module]
392	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
393	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
394	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
395	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
396	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
397	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
398	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
399	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
400	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
401	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
402	  global function.
403	\end{definition}
404	
405	\begin{definition}\leanok
406	[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
407	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
408	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
409	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
410	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
411	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
412	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
413	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
414	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
415	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
416	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
417	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
418	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
419	\end{definition}
420	
421	\begin{lemma}\leanok
422	[Scalar endomorphism of the unit is the identity]
423	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}
424	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_one}
425	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
426	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the unit function \(1 \in
427	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the identity of the unit module:
428	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
429	\end{lemma}
430	
431	\begin{proof}
432	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
433	  Multiplication by \(1\) is the identity on every section, so under the
434	  identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the section \(1\)
435	  corresponds to \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
436	\end{proof}
437	
438	\begin{lemma}\leanok
439	[Scalar endomorphism of the zero function is zero]
440	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_zero}
442	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
443	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the zero function \(0 \in
444	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the zero endomorphism:
445	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\).
446	\end{lemma}
447	
448	\begin{proof}
449	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
450	  Multiplication by \(0\) sends every section to \(0\), so the corresponding
451	  endomorphism of the unit module is the zero map.
452	\end{proof}
453	
454	\begin{definition}
455	
456	\leanok
457	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
458	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
459	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
460	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd}
461	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
462	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
463	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
464	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
465	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
466	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
467	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
468	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
469	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
470	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
471	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
472	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
473	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
474	  \[
475	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
476	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
477	  \]
478	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
479	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
480	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
481	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
482	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
483	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
484	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
485	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
486	
487	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
488	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
489	  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
490	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
491	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
492	  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification
493	  \(\Gamma(\Spec R^I, \mathcal{O}) \cong R^I\); the corresponding scalar
494	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
495	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
496	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
497	  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
498	  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
499	  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
500	  free sheaves'' primitive.
501	\end{definition}
502	
503	\begin{lemma}\leanok
504	[The $I$-columns of the chart quotient are the identity]
505	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
506	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_ιFree}
507	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
508	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
509	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
510	  For each \(k \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), the chart quotient \(u^I\)
511	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) carries the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th basis section of the
512	  free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) -- where \(\iota_I(k)\) is the \(k\)-th
513	  element of \(I\) in increasing order -- to the \(k\)-th basis section of
514	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\); that is, the \(I\)-indexed columns of \(u^I\) form the
515	  identity:
516	  \[
517	    e_{\iota_I(k)} \,\circ\, u^I \;=\; e_k.
518	  \]
519	\end{lemma}
520	
521	\begin{proof}
522	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
523	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
524	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
525	  By the biproduct presentation of \(u^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}), the
526	  composite \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I\) reads off the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th column of the
527	  component matrix, whose \(p\)-th entry is the scalar endomorphism
528	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)})\). The \(I\)-indexed columns of the
529	  universal matrix \(X^I\), read through the order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong
530	  I\), form the \(d \times d\) identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}),
531	  so \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\). Hence the diagonal entry is
532	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the
533	  off-diagonal entries are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\)
534	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the resulting column is the \(k\)-th coordinate
535	  inclusion, giving \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I = e_k\).
536	\end{proof}
537	
538	\begin{lemma}\leanok
539	[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
540	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
541	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
542	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
543	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
544	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
545	\end{lemma}
546	
547	\begin{proof}
548	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
549	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
550	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
551	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
552	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
553	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
554	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
555	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
556	  \[
557	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
558	  \]
559	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
560	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
561	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
562	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
563	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
564	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
565	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
566	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
567	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
568	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
569	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
570	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
571	\end{proof}
572	
573	\begin{definition}
574	
575	\leanok
576	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
577	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
578	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
579	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
580	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
581	    lem:gr_glueData_bridges, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
582	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
583	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
584	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
585	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
586	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
587	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
588	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
589	  %  by putting
590	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
591	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
592	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
593	  \[
594	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
595	  \]
596	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
597	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
598	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
599	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
600	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
601	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
602	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
603	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
604	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
605	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
606	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
607	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
608	\end{definition}
609	
610	\begin{definition}
611	
612	\leanok
613	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
614	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
615	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
616	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
617	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
618	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
619	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
620	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
621	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
622	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
623	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
624	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
625	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
626	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
627	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
628	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
629	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
630	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
631	  \[
632	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
633	  \]
634	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
635	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
636	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
637	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
638	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
639	  \[
640	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
641	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
642	  \]
643	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
644	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
645	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
646	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
647	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
648	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
649	\end{definition}
650	
651	\section{The functor of points}
652	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
653	
654	The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme \(T\) to the set of \emph{equivalence classes} of
655	rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). The following block packages a single such
656	quotient, the equivalence relation on them, and the pullback action through which the
657	functor acts on morphisms; the two coherence blocks after it isolate the comparison
658	identities that make that action functorial.
659	
660	\begin{definition}[Rank-\(d\) quotients and their pullback action]
661	  \label{def:gr_rankQuotient}
662	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient,
663	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.Rel,
664	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_refl,
665	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_symm,
666	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_trans,
667	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqSetoid,
668	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback,
669	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback_rel}
670	  \uses{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
671	        def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
672	  A \emph{rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} on a scheme \(T\) is a triple
673	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules
674	  \(\mathcal{F}\) that is locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank})
675	  together with an epimorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) out
676	  of the trivial rank-\(r\) bundle. Two such quotients \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\)
677	  and \(y = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) are \emph{equivalent}, written \(x \sim y\),
678	  when there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with
679	  \(q \circ f = q'\) (equivalently \(\ker q = \ker q'\)). This relation is reflexive
680	  (\(f = \mathrm{id}\)), symmetric (\(f \mapsto f^{-1}\)), and transitive (composition of the
681	  witnessing isomorphisms), hence an equivalence relation -- the setoid whose quotient is the
682	  functor's value at \(T\).
683	
684	  For a morphism \(\psi : T' \to T\) the \emph{pullback action} sends
685	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) to
686	  \[
687	    \psi^{*}x \;=\;
688	      \bigl\langle\, \psi^{*}\mathcal{F},\;
689	        \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
690	        \psi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \psi^{*}q\ }
691	        \psi^{*}\mathcal{F} \,\bigr\rangle,
692	  \]
693	  re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via the free-pullback isomorphism
694	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. The result is again a rank-\(d\) quotient: the composite is
695	  an epimorphism because pullback, a left adjoint, preserves epimorphisms and the
696	  re-presentation is an isomorphism, and \(\psi^{*}\mathcal{F}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)
697	  by \cref{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}. The pullback action respects the equivalence
698	  relation -- given \(f\) witnessing \(x \sim y\), the pulled-back isomorphism \(\psi^{*}f\)
699	  witnesses \(\psi^{*}x \sim \psi^{*}y\), by functoriality of pullback -- so it descends to a
700	  map of equivalence classes.
701	\end{definition}
702	
703	The two blocks below record the comparison identities, between the free-pullback isomorphism
704	\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} and Mathlib's pseudofunctor structure on pullback, that make
705	the descended pullback action of \cref{def:gr_rankQuotient} functorial.
706	
707	\begin{lemma}[Identity coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
708	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
709	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
710	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
711	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\), the canonical comparison morphism of unit
712	  modules \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) -- the map underlying the
713	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index -- coincides
714	  with the component at \(\mathbf{1}\) of the pullback-identity isomorphism
715	  \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
716	\end{lemma}
717	
718	\begin{proof}
719	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
720	  The comparison morphism is the mate of \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf 1}\) under the
721	  pullback--pushforward adjunction: the image of the identity of \(\mathbf 1\) through the
722	  adjunction's hom-equivalence on the unit. For the identity scheme morphism the pushforward
723	  is the identity functor, so the adjunction unit is itself an identity; Mathlib's
724	  pullback-identity isomorphism is exactly the conjugate (left-adjoint transpose) of the
725	  pushforward-identity isomorphism. Tracing the identity through the hom-equivalence and the
726	  conjugate-transpose identity for the pullback-identity isomorphism shows the comparison
727	  equals the \(\mathbf 1\)-component of that isomorphism.
728	\end{proof}
729	
730	\begin{lemma}[Composite coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
731	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
732	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
733	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
734	  For composable scheme morphisms \(a : T'' \to T'\) and \(b : T' \to T\), the comparison
735	  morphism of unit modules \((b \circ a)^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) factors as
736	  \(a^{*}\) of the comparison for \(b\), the comparison for \(a\), and the component at
737	  \(\mathbf{1}\) of the iterated-pullback comparison \(a^{*}b^{*} \cong (b\circ a)^{*}\)
738	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}).
739	\end{lemma}
740	
741	\begin{proof}
742	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
743	  This is the composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}: the mate
744	  construction is compatible with composition of adjunctions, so the comparison for
745	  \(b \circ a\) is the pasting of the comparisons for \(a\) and \(b\) reassociated by the
746	  pseudofunctor comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. The pentagon coherence of the
747	  iterated-pullback comparisons makes the factorisation well-defined.
748	\end{proof}
749	
750	Together \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id} and \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
751	upgrade to the corresponding free-sheaf coherences -- the free-pullback isomorphism at the
752	identity equals the component of the pullback-identity isomorphism, and likewise for the
753	composite -- by coproduct extensionality over the decomposition
754	\(\mathcal{O}^{\,\oplus I} = \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\). These coherences discharge both
755	functoriality laws (\(\mathrm{map\_id}\) and \(\mathrm{map\_comp}\)) of the Grassmannian
756	functor \cref{def:grassmannian_functor}.
757	
758	\begin{definition}
759	
760	\leanok
761	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
762	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
763	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
764	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor, def:gr_rankQuotient,
765	    lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
766	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
767	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
768	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
769	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
770	  % "represents the
771	  %  contravariant functor
772	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
773	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
774	  %  set of all equivalence classes
775	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
776	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
777	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
778	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
779	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
780	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
781	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
782	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
783	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
784	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
785	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
786	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
787	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
788	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
789	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
790	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
791	  \[
792	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
793	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
794	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
795	  \]
796	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
797	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
798	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
799	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
800	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
801	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
802	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
803	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
804	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
805	\end{definition}
806	
807	\section{The universal property}
808	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
809	
810	\begin{theorem}
811	
812	\leanok
813	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
814	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
815	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
816	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
817	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
818	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
819	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
820	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
821	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
822	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
823	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
824	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
825	  %  contravariant functor
826	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
827	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
828	  %  set of all equivalence classes
829	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
830	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
831	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
832	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
833	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
834	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
835	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
836	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
837	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
838	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
839	  \[
840	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
841	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
842	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
843	    \qquad
844	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
845	      \;\longmapsto\;
846	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
847	  \]
848	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
849	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
850	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
851	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
852	  universal family.
853	\end{theorem}
854	
855	\begin{proof}
856	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
857	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
858	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
859	    def:grassmannian_functor}
860	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
861	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
862	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
863	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
864	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
865	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
866	
867	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
868	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
869	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
870	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
871	  the composite
872	  \[
873	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
874	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
875	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
876	  \]
877	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
878	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
879	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
880	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
881	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
882	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
883	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
884	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
885	
886	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
887	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
888	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
889	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
890	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
891	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
892	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
893	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
894	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
895	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
896	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
897	
898	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
899	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
900	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
901	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
902	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
903	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
904	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
905	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
906	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
907	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
908	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
909	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
910	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
911	
912	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
913	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
914	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
915	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
916	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
917	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
918	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
919	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
920	
921	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
922	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
923	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
924	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
925	\end{proof}

1901	  \(x\). Since \(s \in D(f)\), the element \(f\) acts invertibly on the local ring
1902	  \(\OO_{S,s}\) and hence on \(\F_x\); thus \(\F_x\) is equally a localization of the
1903	  inverted module \((M_j)_f\), and \(\OO_{S,s}\) is the localization of \(A_f\) at the
1904	  prime of \(s\) (a point of \(D(f) = \Spec A_f\)). By
1905	  \cref{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat} the module \((M_j)_f\) is flat over \(A_f\), and
1906	  localization preserves flatness (\cref{lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves}), so
1907	  \(\F_x\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,s} = \OO_{S,\,p(x)}\).
1908	\end{proof}
1909	
1910	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1911	[G3.3 — flatness over a local base is local on the source affine]
1912	  \label{lem:gf_flat_base_local_on_source}
1913	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_base_local_on_source}
1914	  \uses{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_maximal}
1915	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(B\) an \(R\)-algebra, and \(N\) a \(B\)-module,
1916	  regarded as an \(R\)-module through the scalar tower \(R \to B \to N\). Suppose
1917	  that for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak q \subset B\) the localization
1918	  \(N_{\mathfrak q}\) is flat over \(R\). Then \(N\) is flat over \(R\).
1919	\end{lemma}
1920	
1921	\begin{proof}
1922	  \uses{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_maximal}
1923	  This is the source-locality criterion for flatness over a fixed base ring,
1924	  instantiated with the canonical localizations \(N_{\mathfrak q}\) of \(N\) at the
1925	  maximal ideals \(\mathfrak q\) of \(B\): each \(N_{\mathfrak q}\) is a localized
1926	  module of \(N\) at the prime \(\mathfrak q\) of the source ring \(B\), and the
1927	  hypothesis supplies the required flatness of every \(N_{\mathfrak q}\) over \(R\).
1928	  \Cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_maximal} then yields that \(N\) is flat over
1929	  \(R\). \emph{(Note: unlike \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_localized_maximal}, which
1930	  localizes over the same ring it tests flatness over, here \(N\) is localized at the
1931	  primes of the source ring \(B\) while flatness is tested over the distinct base
1932	  ring \(R\).)}
1933	\end{proof}
1934	
1935	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1936	[G3.4 — stalk flatness over the local base via transitivity]
1937	  \label{lem:gf_stalk_flat_localBase}
1938	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_stalk_flat_localBase}
1939	  \uses{lem:gf_stalk_flat_over_base, lem:mathlib_localization_flat, lem:mathlib_flat_trans}
1940	  In the situation of \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}, let \(x \in V = D(f)\) and
1941	  let \(y \in p^{-1}(V)\) be a source point with \(p(y)\) a generization of \(x\).
1942	  Then the stalk \(\F_y\) is flat over the local ring \(\OO_{S,x}\).
1943	\end{lemma}
1944	
1945	\begin{proof}
1946	  \uses{lem:gf_stalk_flat_over_base, lem:mathlib_localization_flat, lem:mathlib_flat_trans}
1947	  By \cref{lem:gf_stalk_flat_over_base}, \(\F_y\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,\,p(y)}\). As
1948	  \(p(y)\) is a generization of \(x\), the comparison map
1949	  \(\OO_{S,x} \to \OO_{S,\,p(y)}\) is a localization, hence \(\OO_{S,\,p(y)}\) is flat
1950	  over \(\OO_{S,x}\) (\cref{lem:mathlib_localization_flat}). Transitivity of flatness
1951	  along \(\OO_{S,x} \to \OO_{S,\,p(y)} \to \F_y\)
1952	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_flat_trans}) upgrades ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,\,p(y)}\)'' to
1953	  ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,x}\)''.
1954	\end{proof}
1955	
1956	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1957	[B1.0 — localization commutes with base tensor]
1958	  \label{lem:gf_localizedModule_baseChange_tensor_comm}
1959	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_localizedModule_baseChange_tensor_comm}
1960	  Let \(R \to B\) be a ring map, \(T \subseteq B\) a submonoid, \(N\) a \(B\)-module,
1961	  and \(K\) an \(R\)-module. There is an isomorphism, natural in \(K\),
1962	  \[
1963	    \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,(N \otimes_R K)
1964	      \;\cong\; (\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K .
1965	  \]
1966	\end{lemma}
1967	\begin{proof}
1968	  Localization is base change: \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,M \cong T^{-1}B \otimes_B M\)
1969	  naturally in the \(B\)-module \(M\). Applying this to \(M = N \otimes_R K\) and using
1970	  associativity/commutativity of the tensor product over the tower
1971	  \(R \to B \to T^{-1}B\) gives
1972	  \(T^{-1}B \otimes_B (N \otimes_R K) \cong (T^{-1}B \otimes_B N) \otimes_R K
1973	  \cong (\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K\); naturality in \(K\) is inherited
1974	  from the tensor functor.
1975	\end{proof}
1976	
1977	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1978	[B1 — localizing the source ring preserves flatness over the base]
1979	  \label{lem:gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase}
1980	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase}
1981	  \uses{lem:gf_localizedModule_baseChange_tensor_comm}
1982	  Let \(R \to B \to N\) be a scalar tower in which \(N\) is a \(B\)-module, and let
1983	  \(T \subseteq B\) be a submonoid of the \emph{source} ring \(B\). View \(N\) as an
1984	  \(R\)-module through the tower. If \(N\) is flat over \(R\), then the localized module
1985	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N\) is again flat over \(R\). \emph{(Note: the existing
1986	  Mathlib results \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves} localize over a
1987	  submonoid of the \emph{base} \(R\); here the submonoid lives in the source \(B\) while
1988	  the base \(R\) is untouched, so a separate argument is required.)}
1989	\end{lemma}
1990	
1991	\begin{proof}
1992	  \uses{lem:gf_localizedModule_baseChange_tensor_comm}
1993	  Localization at \(T\) commutes with tensoring against an \(R\)-module: by
1994	  \cref{lem:gf_localizedModule_baseChange_tensor_comm}, for every \(R\)-module \(K\)
1995	  there is a natural isomorphism
1996	  \[
1997	    \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,(N \otimes_R K)
1998	      \;\cong\; (\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K,
1999	  \]
2000	  natural in \(K\) (compatible with the maps induced by any \(R\)-linear \(K \to K'\)).
2001	  Flatness of \(N\) over \(R\) means that for every injection \(K \hookrightarrow K'\) of
2002	  \(R\)-modules the induced map \(N \otimes_R K \to N \otimes_R K'\) is injective.
2003	  Localization at the submonoid \(T\) is an exact functor, so it preserves this
2004	  injectivity; transporting along the natural isomorphism turns the result into
2005	  injectivity of
2006	  \((\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K \to (\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K'\).
2007	  As this holds for every injection of \(R\)-modules, \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N\)
2008	  is flat over \(R\).
2009	\end{proof}

2011	\begin{lemma}[Mathlib anchor — basic open under restriction]
2012	  \label{lem:mathlib_scheme_basicOpen_res}
2013	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.basicOpen_res}
2014	  \mathlibok
2015	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme}).}
2016	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(V \le U\) opens of \(X\), and \(s \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Restricting
2017	  \(s\) to \(V\) meets its basic open with \(V\):
2018	  \[
2019	    X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,(s|_V) \;=\; V \sqcap X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,s .
2020	  \]
2021	\end{lemma}
2022	
2023	\begin{lemma}[B2.1 — cross-chart basic-open identity]
2024	  \label{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2025	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2026	  \uses{lem:mathlib_scheme_basicOpen_res}
2027	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(W, W_j \subseteq X\) two opens with overlap
2028	  \(O = W \sqcap W_j\), and let \(g \in \Gamma(X, W)\), \(\bar g \in \Gamma(X, W_j)\) be sections
2029	  that restrict to the \emph{same} section over \(O\), i.e.\ \(g|_O = \bar g|_O\) in
2030	  \(\Gamma(X, O)\). Suppose both basic opens lie in the overlap,
2031	  \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g \le O\) and \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g \le O\). Then they agree as
2032	  opens of \(X\):
2033	  \[
2034	    X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g \;=\; X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g .
2035	  \]
2036	  In particular the open \(D(g)\) is computed identically whether read in the chart \(W\) or in
2037	  the chart \(W_j\), and \(D(g) = D(\bar g) \le W_j\) (no module content is involved).
2038	\end{lemma}
2039	
2040	\begin{proof}
2041	  \uses{lem:mathlib_scheme_basicOpen_res}
2042	  Basic opens are compatible with restriction (\cref{lem:mathlib_scheme_basicOpen_res}). For the
2043	  inclusion \(O \le W\), \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,(g|_O) = O \sqcap X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g\), and
2044	  since \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g \le O\) the meet collapses to
2045	  \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,(g|_O) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g\). Symmetrically, for \(O \le W_j\),
2046	  \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,(\bar g|_O) = O \sqcap X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g
2047	  = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\). The hypothesis \(g|_O = \bar g|_O\) makes the two left-hand
2048	  sides equal, so \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\).
2049	\end{proof}
2050	
2051	\begin{lemma}[B2.2 — two-leg section-localization transport]
2052	  \label{lem:gf_section_localization_twoleg}
2053	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_section_localization_twoleg}
2054	  \uses{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen,
2055	        lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
2056	  In the situation of \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}, fix an affine open
2057	  \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\), a patch \(W_j = \Spec B_j\) with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and a matched
2058	  pair \(g \in \Gamma(X, W)\), \(\bar g \in B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) cutting out the same basic open
2059	  \(D := X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g \le W \sqcap W_j\) (as produced by
2060	  \cref{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}). Then the single group \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) carries two
2061	  localized-module structures:
2062	  \begin{enumerate}
2063	    \item the section restriction \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D)\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) as
2064	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\); and
2065	    \item the section restriction \(M_j \to \Gamma(\F, D)\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) as
2066	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,\bar g)^{-1}\,M_j = (M_j)_{\bar g}\).
2067	  \end{enumerate}
2068	  Consequently \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W) \cong (M_j)_{\bar g}\) canonically, by
2069	  uniqueness of localized modules.
2070	\end{lemma}
2071	
2072	\begin{proof}
2073	  \uses{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen,
2074	        lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
2075	  By \cref{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq} the two recipes for the open agree,
2076	  \(D = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\), so \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) is one and the
2077	  same group whether \(D\) is read in chart \(W\) or in chart \(W_j\). Applying the quasi-coherent
2078	  section-localization datum \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} with ambient affine \(W\)
2079	  and element \(g\), the restriction \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D)\) is the localization at
2080	  \(\mathrm{powers}\,g\), giving (1). Applying the same datum with ambient affine \(W_j\) and element
2081	  \(\bar g\) (legitimate since \(D = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g \le W_j\)), the restriction
2082	  \(M_j \to \Gamma(\F, D)\) is the localization at \(\mathrm{powers}\,\bar g\), giving (2). Both maps
2083	  localize their respective sources onto the same target \(\Gamma(\F, D)\); the universal property of
2084	  localized modules (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule.iso}\) uniqueness) supplies the canonical isomorphism
2085	  \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W) \cong (M_j)_{\bar g}\). Finiteness of \(M_j\) over \(B_j\)
2086	  is recorded by \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}.
2087	\end{proof}
2088	
2089	\begin{lemma}[B2.3 — base comparison: \(\Gamma(S,U)\) localizes \(A_f\)]
2090	  \label{lem:gf_base_localization_comparison}
2091	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_base_localization_comparison}
2092	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2093	  In the situation of \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}, let \(U \le V = D(f) \subseteq U_0 = \Spec A\)
2094	  be an affine open of \(S\). Then \(A_f = \Gamma(S, D(f))\) is the away-localization of \(A\) at
2095	  \(f\), and the restriction \(A_f \to R := \Gamma(S, U)\) realises \(R\) as a localization of \(A_f\)
2096	  (both are rings of fractions of the domain \(A\) inside its fraction field, \(R\) inverting at least
2097	  the denominators already inverted in \(A_f\)).
2098	\end{lemma}
2099	
2100	\begin{proof}
2101	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2102	  Since \(D(f)\) is a basic open of the affine \(U_0 = \Spec A\), the restriction
2103	  \(A = \Gamma(S, U_0) \to \Gamma(S, D(f))\) exhibits \(A_f = \Gamma(S, D(f))\) as the
2104	  away-localization of \(A\) at \(f\) (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}). The affine open
2105	  \(U \le D(f) = \Spec A_f\) of the integral scheme \(S\) has section ring
2106	  \(R = \Gamma(S, U) \subseteq \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) containing \(A_f\); the comparison
2107	  \(A_f \to R\) is the restriction along \(U \le D(f)\) and exhibits \(R\) as a localization of
2108	  \(A_f\) inside the fraction field of the domain \(A\).
2109	\end{proof}
2110	
2111	\begin{lemma}[B2 — chart-independent section-localization on a basic open]
2112	  \label{lem:gf_section_localization_flat_descent}
2113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_section_localization_flat_descent}
2114	  \uses{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq, lem:gf_section_localization_twoleg,
2115	        lem:gf_base_localization_comparison}
2116	  In the situation of \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}, fix an affine open
2117	  \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), a patch \(W_j = \Spec B_j\) of the cover, and an element
2118	  \(g \in \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(D(g) \subseteq W \cap W_j\). Then:
2119	  \begin{enumerate}
2120	    \item the section restriction \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) exhibits
2121	      \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) as the away-localization
2122	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\) of the source module
2123	      \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at \(g\); and
2124	    \item independently of the chart, \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) \cong (M_j)_{\bar g}\) is the
2125	      localization of the patch module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\) at the image
2126	      \(\bar g \in B_j\) of \(g\).
2127	  \end{enumerate}
2128	  Moreover the base restriction \(\Gamma(S, U) \to A_f\) identifies \(\Gamma(S, U)\) and
2129	  \(A_f\) as localizations of \(A\) (both inverting the relevant denominators of the
2130	  domain \(A\)), so that \(\Gamma(S, U)\) is a localization of \(A_f\).
2131	\end{lemma}
2132	
2133	\begin{proof}
2134	  \uses{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq, lem:gf_section_localization_twoleg,
2135	        lem:gf_base_localization_comparison}
2136	  The element \(g \in \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(D(g) \subseteq W \cap W_j\) has image
2137	  \(\bar g \in B_j\) cutting out the same basic open
2138	  \(D(g) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\) (\cref{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}). The two-leg
2139	  transport \cref{lem:gf_section_localization_twoleg} then yields statements~(1) and~(2)
2140	  verbatim: the restriction \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\)
2141	  as \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\), and chart-independently
2142	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) \cong (M_j)_{\bar g}\). The base comparison
2143	  \cref{lem:gf_base_localization_comparison} supplies the final sentence: with
2144	  \(U \le V = D(f)\), the ring \(\Gamma(S, U)\) is a localization of \(A_f = \Gamma(S, D(f))\)
2145	  inside \(\mathrm{Frac}(A)\).
2146	\end{proof}
