2148	\begin{lemma}[B2.4 — finite spanning basic-open cover aligned to the patches]
2149	  \label{lem:gf_crossChart_spanning_cover}
2150	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_crossChart_spanning_cover}
2151	  \uses{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover, lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2152	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open, and \(\{W_j\}_{j}\) a finite family of
2153	  affine opens of \(X\) whose union contains \(W\). Then there is a finite index set \(K\) and, for
2154	  each \(k \in K\), a chart index \(j(k)\) together with a matched pair \(g_k \in \Gamma(X, W)\),
2155	  \(\bar g_k \in \Gamma(X, W_{j(k)})\) that agree over the overlap \(W \sqcap W_{j(k)}\) and cut out a
2156	  common basic open
2157	  \[
2158	    D(g_k) \;=\; X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g_k \;\le\; W \sqcap W_{j(k)},
2159	  \]
2160	  such that \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,\{g_k : k \in K\} = \top\) in \(\Gamma(X, W)\) (equivalently, the
2161	  \(D(g_k)\) cover \(W\)). This is the spanning family fed to
2162	  \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span}.
2163	\end{lemma}
2164	
2165	\begin{proof}
2166	  \uses{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover, lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2167	  Inside each overlap \(W \cap W_j\) — open in both affines \(W\) and \(W_j\) — the opens that are
2168	  simultaneously basic in \(W\) and basic in \(W_j\) form a basis of the subspace topology: given
2169	  \(x \in W \cap W_j\), choose \(b \in \Gamma(X, W_j)\) with \(x \in D(b) \subseteq W \cap W_j\) and
2170	  then \(g \in \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(x \in D(g) \subseteq D(b)\); shrinking \(b\) to the common open
2171	  and recording its restriction over the overlap produces a basic open of \(W\) that is matched, over
2172	  \(W \cap W_j\), by a section \(\bar g \in \Gamma(X, W_j)\) (\cref{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2173	  certifies \(D(g) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\)). Ranging over all \(j\), and because the \(W_j\)
2174	  cover \(W\), these common basic opens form an open cover of the affine \(W\). The affine \(W\) is
2175	  quasi-compact, so a finite subfamily already covers it: applying the standard-subcover extraction
2176	  \cref{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover} to this open cover of \(W\) returns a finite family
2177	  \(\{g_k\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,\{g_k\} = \top\), each \(D(g_k)\) a
2178	  common basic open contained in some \(W \cap W_{j(k)}\) with matching
2179	  \(\bar g_k \in \Gamma(X, W_{j(k)})\).
2180	\end{proof}
2181	
2182	\begin{lemma}[G3 — flat-locality assembly from per-patch freeness]
2183	  \label{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
2184	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_locality_assembly}
2185	  \uses{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat, lem:gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase,
2186	        lem:gf_section_localization_flat_descent, lem:gf_crossChart_spanning_cover,
2187	        lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span,
2188	        lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves, lem:mathlib_flat_of_free,
2189	        lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2190	  Let \(p : X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism with \(S\) integral and locally
2191	  noetherian, and \(\F\) a finite-type quasi-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Fix a non-empty
2192	  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
2193	  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
2194	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
2195	  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
2196	  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every
2197	  affine open \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), the section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat
2198	  over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).
2199	\end{lemma}
2200	
2201	\begin{proof}
2202	  \uses{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat, lem:gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase,
2203	        lem:gf_section_localization_flat_descent, lem:gf_crossChart_spanning_cover,
2204	        lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span,
2205	        lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves, lem:mathlib_flat_of_free,
2206	        lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2207	  Flatness is established by a \emph{source-span} descent on the affine \(W\), following the
2208	  standard flat-locality argument of \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 (cf.\ Hartshorne,
2209	  \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.9, flat families): instead of testing stalkwise, we
2210	  descend along a finite cover of \(W\) by basic opens aligned to the patches.
2211	
2212	  Fix affine \(U \le V = D(f)\) and \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\), and write \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\),
2213	  \(B = \Gamma(X, W)\), \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\); \(N\) is a \(B\)-module and \(B\) an
2214	  \(R\)-algebra. By \cref{lem:gf_section_localization_flat_descent}, \(R\) is a
2215	  localization of \(A_f\).
2216	
2217	  \emph{A spanning family of basic opens.} By the patch-aligned covering lemma
2218	  \cref{lem:gf_crossChart_spanning_cover}, applied to the affine \(W\) and the finite affine cover
2219	  \(\{W_j\}\) of \(p^{-1}(U) \supseteq W\), there is a finite family
2220	  \(\{g_k\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,\{g_k\} = \top\) (so the \(D(g_k)\)
2221	  cover \(W\)), where each \(D(g_k) \subseteq W \cap W_{j(k)}\) is a common basic open matched by a
2222	  patch element \(\bar g_k \in B_{j(k)}\). The chosen \(g_k\) span the unit ideal of \(B\).
2223	
2224	  \emph{Each cover piece is flat over \(R\).} Fix such a \(g\), lying over the patch
2225	  \(W_j\). The per-patch freeness gives \((M_j)_f\) free, hence flat over \(A_f\)
2226	  (\cref{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat}, \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_free}); localizing the base
2227	  from \(A_f\) to \(R\) preserves flatness (\cref{lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves}),
2228	  so the corresponding module is flat over \(R\). Localizing this further at the source
2229	  submonoid \((\mathrm{powers}\,\bar g) \subseteq B_j\) keeps \(R\)-flatness by the
2230	  source-localization lemma \cref{lem:gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase}. Since
2231	  \(D(g) \subseteq W \cap W_j\) lies over \(U \le V = D(f)\), the image of \(f\) is already
2232	  invertible in \((B_j)_{\bar g}\); hence localizing \((M_j)_f\) further at
2233	  \((\mathrm{powers}\,\bar g)\) inverts nothing new on the base side and
2234	  \((M_j)_f\) localized at \(\bar g\) coincides with
2235	  \((M_j)_{\bar g} = \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,(\mathrm{powers}\,\bar g)\,M_j\)
2236	  (transitivity of localization). By
2237	  \cref{lem:gf_section_localization_flat_descent} this module is exactly
2238	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) \cong (M_j)_{\bar g}\). Hence each \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is flat over
2239	  \(R\).
2240	
2241	  \emph{Span descent.} By \cref{lem:gf_section_localization_flat_descent} each
2242	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is the away-localization of \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\) at \(g\), and the
2243	  \(g\)'s span the unit ideal of the source ring \(B\). The source-span flatness criterion
2244	  \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span}, with base \(R\), source \(B\), and these
2245	  flat principal localizations, yields that \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over
2246	  \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
2247	\end{proof}
2248	
2249	
2250	\begin{theorem}
2251	
2252	
2253	
2254	\leanok
2255	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
2256	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
2257	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
2258	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
2259	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
2260	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
2261	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2262	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
2263	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
2264	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
2265	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
2266	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
2267	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
2268	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
2269	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
2270	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
2271	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
2272	
2273	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
2274	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
2275	\end{theorem}
2276	
2277	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
2278	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
2279	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
2280	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
2281	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
2282	% by restricting to a non-empty
2283	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
2284	\begin{proof}
2285	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
2286	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
2287	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
2288	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
2289	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
2290	
2291	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact (by
2292	  the \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\,p]\) hypothesis) and \(U_0\) is affine hence
2293	  quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is quasi-compact; choose a
2294	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
2295	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
2296	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
2297	
2298	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
2299	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
2300	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
2301	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
2302	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
2303	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
2304	
2305	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
2306	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
2307	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
2308	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
2309	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
2310	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
2311	
2312	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
2313	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the flat-locality assembly
2314	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly} promotes these per-patch freeness statements to the
2315	  flatness conclusion by a \emph{source-span} descent: over any affine \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\)
2316	  one covers \(W\) by finitely many basic opens \(D(g) \subseteq W \cap W_j\) aligned to the
2317	  patches, propagates \(R\)-flatness from \((M_j)_f\) to each section module
2318	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) via the source-localization lemma, and descends along the spanning
2319	  family \(\{g\}\) of \(\Gamma(X, W)\) using \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span}.
2320	  This produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
2321	\end{proof}
2322

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
5	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
6	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
7	
8	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
9	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
10	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
11	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
12	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
13	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
14	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
15	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
16	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
17	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
18	
19	\begin{enumerate}
20	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
21	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
22	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
23	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
24	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
25	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
26	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
27	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
28	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
29	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
30	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
31	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
32	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
33	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
34	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
35	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
36	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
37	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
38	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
39	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
40	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
41	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
42	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
43	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
44	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
45	    \emph{module} structure on
46	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
47	\end{enumerate}
48	
49	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
50	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
51	
52	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
53	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
54	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
55	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
56	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
57	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
58	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
59	monoidal structure applies.
60	
61	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
62	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
63	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
64	  \mathlibok
65	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
66	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
67	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
68	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
69	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
70	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
71	  and an object \(U\),
72	  \[
73	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
74	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
75	  \]
76	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
77	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
78	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
79	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
80	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
81	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
82	\end{lemma}
83	
84	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
85	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
86	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
87	  \mathlibok
88	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
89	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
90	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
91	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
92	  \[
93	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
94	  \]
95	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
96	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
97	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
98	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
99	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
100	\end{lemma}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
103	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
104	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
107	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
108	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
109	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
110	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
111	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
112	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
113	  the section graded ring.
114	\end{lemma}
115	
116	\begin{definition}\leanok
117	[Presheaf-of-modules category in monoidal form]
118	  \label{def:monoidalPresheaf}
119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.MonoidalPresheaf}
120	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
121	  For a scheme \(X\), write \(\PshMod_{\otimes}(X)\) for the category of presheaves
122	  of modules over the underlying presheaf of (plain) rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\),
123	  obtained by composing the structure sheaf with the forgetful functor
124	  \(\mathrm{CommRingCat} \to \mathrm{RingCat}\). This is definitionally
125	  \(X.\PshMod\), but presented in the exact form for which Mathlib equips it with
126	  the symmetric monoidal structure of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib};
127	  it is the carrier on which the objectwise tensor product of
128	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} is taken.
129	\end{definition}
130	
131	\begin{definition}\leanok
132	[Scheme-level module sheafification functor]
133	  \label{def:schemeModuleSheafification}
134	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafification}
135	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
136	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{module sheafification functor}
137	  \((-)^{\#} : X.\PshMod \to X.\Modules\) is Mathlib's sheafification functor
138	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) specialised to the identity
139	  morphism of the underlying presheaf of rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (a locally
140	  bijective map). Applying it to the objectwise tensor presheaf is what defines the
141	  sheaf tensor product \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}.
142	\end{definition}
143	
144	\begin{definition}\leanok
145	[Unit module of a scheme]
146	  \label{def:unitModule}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitModule}
148	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
149	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{unit module} \(\mathbf{1}_X \in X.\Modules\) is the
150	  structure sheaf viewed as a module over itself, i.e.\ Mathlib's
151	  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.unit}\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) for
152	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It is the value of the zeroth tensor power,
153	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}).
154	\end{definition}
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
158	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
160	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
161	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
162	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
163	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
164	  % L2282-L2297).
165	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
166	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
167	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
168	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
169	  %  $$
170	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
171	  %  $$
172	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
173	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
174	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
175	  %  Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
176	  %  as the sheafification of the above:
177	  %  $$
178	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
179	  %  =
180	  %  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
181	  %  $$"
182	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CA (tensor
183	  product).}
184	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Their
185	  \emph{tensor product} is the sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
186	  \[
187	    \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
188	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\bigr)^{\#},
189	  \]
190	  the sheafification (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of the
191	  tensor product presheaf \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\),
192	  whose value on an open \(U\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module
193	  \(\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)\). Concretely, the
194	  tensor product presheaf is the Mathlib presheaf-level monoidal product
195	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) of the underlying presheaves of
196	  modules of \(\mathcal{F}\) and \(\mathcal{G}\), and
197	  \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) is its sheafification. This
198	  construction is symmetric and associative because the presheaf tensor product
199	  is, and sheafification preserves the coherence isomorphisms.
200	\end{definition}
201	
202	\begin{definition}\leanok
203	[Tensor power of a line bundle]
204	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}
206	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
207	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
208	  % `definition-powers' (Tag 01CU) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
209	  % L4220-L4228).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
211	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
212	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
213	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
214	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
215	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
216	  %  exactly $n$ times."
217	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
218	  powers).}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
220	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
221	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
222	  on \(m\):
223	  \[
224	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
225	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
226	    \qquad
227	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
228	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
229	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
230	  \]
231	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
232	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
233	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
234	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
235	  invertible.
236	\end{definition}
237	
238	\begin{lemma}\leanok
239	[Zeroth tensor power is the unit]
240	  \label{lem:tensorPow_zero}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_zero}
242	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:unitModule}
243	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
244	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\), the unit module
245	  (\cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of the recursive definition
246	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
247	\end{lemma}
248	
249	\begin{lemma}\leanok
250	[Tensor-power recursion step]
251	  \label{lem:tensorPow_succ}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_succ}
253	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
254	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) and \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) one has
255	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
256	  \mathcal{L}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); this is the successor clause of
257	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
258	\end{lemma}
259	
260	\begin{definition}\leanok
261	[Twist of a coherent sheaf by a tensor power]
262	  \label{def:sheafModuleTwist}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow}
264	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:sheafTensorPow}
265	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
266	  a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\)) and \(\mathcal{L}\) a line bundle. For
267	  \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the \emph{\(m\)-twist} of \(\mathcal{F}\) by
268	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is
269	  \[
270	    \mathcal{F}(m) \;:=\;
271	      \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}
272	      \qquad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}),
273	  \]
274	  the degree-\(m\) carrier of the section graded module. By construction
275	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{O}_X \cong
276	  \mathcal{F}\).
277	\end{definition}
278	
279	\begin{lemma}\leanok
280	[Zeroth twist]
281	  \label{lem:moduleTensorPow_zero}
282	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow_zero}
283	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule,
284	    def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
285	  For sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
286	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathbf{1}_X\)
287	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of
288	  \cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}, holding by definition, and is canonically
289	  isomorphic to \(\mathcal{F}\) via the right unitor
290	  (\cref{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}).
291	\end{lemma}
292	
293	The next four isomorphisms are the parts of the (would-be) symmetric monoidal
294	structure on \(X.\Modules\) that descend through sheafification from
295	\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} using only \emph{functoriality} of the
296	sheafification functor (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) and, for the
297	unitors, the reflective counit isomorphism --- no strong monoidality of
298	sheafification is required. They are the structural inputs of the tensor-power
299	comparison \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.

358	The strong-monoidality comparison \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
359	below is assembled from three reduction lemmas --- a localization criterion, the
360	local-isomorphism property of the unit, and their corollary --- together with the
361	coequalizer presentation of the relative module-presheaf tensor product, the single
362	Mathlib-absent brick on which the comparison rests.

364	\begin{lemma}\leanok
365	[Localization criterion for module sheafification]
366	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
367	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
368	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
369	  For a morphism \(f : \mathcal{P} \to \mathcal{Q}\) of presheaves of
370	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the sheafification \((f)^{\#}\)
371	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules if
372	  and only if the underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves lies in the
373	  weak-equivalence class \(J.W\) of the opens topology \(J\) on \(X\) (i.e.\ is a
374	  local isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves).
375	\end{lemma}
376	
377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
379	  \leanok
380	  Module sheafification (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is the localization
381	  functor at the class \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying
382	  abelian-presheaf morphism lies in \(J.W\), i.e.\ the inverse image of \(J.W\) along
383	  the forgetful functor. The localization criterion for a reflective adjunction
384	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) then gives the stated
385	  equivalence.
386	\end{proof}

388	\begin{lemma}\leanok
389	[The sheafification unit is an abelian local isomorphism]
390	  \label{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
391	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
392	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
393	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the underlying
394	  abelian-group presheaf morphism of the sheafification unit
395	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\)
396	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) is the abelian sheafification
397	  unit \(\mathcal{P} \to a(\mathcal{P})\); it therefore lies in the weak-equivalence
398	  class \(J.W\) of the opens topology.
399	\end{lemma}
400	
401	\begin{proof}
402	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
403	  \leanok
404	  The forgetful functor to abelian-group presheaves carries the module
405	  sheafification unit to the unit of the abelian associated-sheaf adjunction, which
406	  is a local isomorphism, hence a member of \(J.W\).
407	\end{proof}

412	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_unit}

428	\begin{lemma}[Abelian universal property of the relative tensor product]
429	  \label{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
430	  \lean{TensorProduct.liftAddHom}
431	  \mathlibok
432	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorProduct.Basic}).}
433	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N, P\) be \(S\)-modules (or, more
434	  generally, the abelian-group statement: \(M, N\) modules and \(P\) an additive
435	  commutative group). An additive-bilinear map
436	  \(f : M \to (N \to P)\) that is \emph{\(S\)-balanced}, i.e.\ satisfies
437	  \(f(s \cdot m)(n) = f(m)(s \cdot n)\) for all \(s \in S\), factors uniquely
438	  through the relative tensor product as an additive group homomorphism
439	  \(\widetilde{f} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with
440	  \(\widetilde{f}(m \otimes_S n) = f(m)(n)\). This is the abelian-group universal
441	  property of \(M \otimes_S N\): it is the quotient of \(M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N\)
442	  by the \(S\)-balancing relations.
443	\end{lemma}
444	
445	\begin{lemma}[The relative tensor of modules is a coequalizer of abelian groups]
446	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
447	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.isColimitCofork,
448	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actN,
449	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actM,
450	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap,
451	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap,
452	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL,
453	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_surjective,
454	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_comp_act,
455	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aL,
456	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aR,
457	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor,
458	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_epi,
459	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.coeq_condition,
460	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.cofork,
461	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom,
462	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descMor,
463	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descFac}
464	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
465	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N\) be \(S\)-modules. The abelian group
466	  \(M \otimes_S N\) is the coequalizer, \emph{in the category
467	  \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of abelian groups}, of the two parallel
468	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear \(S\)-action maps
469	  \[
470	    a_L,\, a_R :
471	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N)
472	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
473	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N,
474	  \]
475	  where the \emph{left} action map sends \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto
476	  (s \cdot m) \otimes n\) and the \emph{right} action map sends
477	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n)\). The coforking map is
478	  the canonical projection \(\pi : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to M \otimes_S N\),
479	  \(m \otimes n \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
480	\end{lemma}
481	
482	\begin{proof}
483	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
484	  Write \(a_N : S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to N\) and
485	  \(a_M : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} S \to M\) for the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear maps
486	  induced by the scalar action (\(s \otimes n \mapsto s \cdot n\),
487	  \(m \otimes s \mapsto s \cdot m\)); the right action map is
488	  \(a_R = \mathrm{id}_M \otimes a_N\) and the left action map is
489	  \(a_L = (a_M \otimes \mathrm{id}_N) \circ \mathrm{assoc}^{-1}\), where
490	  \(\mathrm{assoc}\) is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor associator. The projection
491	  \(\pi\) is the additive homomorphism produced by the abelian universal property
492	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} from the \(S\)-balanced bilinear map
493	  \((m, n) \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
494	
495	  \emph{Cofork condition.} On an elementary tensor
496	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n)\) we have
497	  \(\pi(a_L(m \otimes s \otimes n)) = (s \cdot m) \otimes_S n
498	  = m \otimes_S (s \cdot n) = \pi(a_R(m \otimes s \otimes n))\), the middle
499	  equality being the defining \(S\)-balancing relation of \(M \otimes_S N\); since
500	  elementary tensors generate, \(\pi \circ a_L = \pi \circ a_R\).
501	
502	  \emph{Universal property.} Given any abelian group \(P\) and a homomorphism
503	  \(g : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to P\) with \(g \circ a_L = g \circ a_R\), the
504	  bilinear map \((m, n) \mapsto g(m \otimes n)\) is \(S\)-balanced (that is exactly
505	  the cofork condition read on elementary tensors), so by
506	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} it factors as a unique
507	  \(\widetilde{g} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi = g\).
508	  Existence is this descended map; uniqueness follows because \(\pi\) is
509	  surjective, hence an epimorphism of abelian groups, so \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi
510	  = g\) determines \(\widetilde{g}\). Packaging existence and uniqueness gives the
511	  colimit cofork.
512	
513	  The named members realise these steps in the category \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\):
514	  \(a_N, a_M\) are the action maps; \(a_L, a_R\) (\emph{actLmap}, \emph{actRmap})
515	  the two rows; \(\pi\) (\emph{projL}, surjective by \emph{projL\_surjective}) the
516	  cofork map; \emph{aL}, \emph{aR}, \emph{piMor} their packaging as abelian-group
517	  morphisms (\emph{piMor} an epimorphism, \emph{piMor\_epi}); \emph{coeq\_condition}
518	  (from the elementwise \emph{projL\_comp\_act}) the cofork condition;
519	  \emph{cofork} the cofork itself; \emph{descHom}/\emph{descMor} the descended map
520	  with factorisation \emph{descFac}; and \emph{isColimitCofork} the assembled
521	  universal property. The auxiliary \(*\_{\mathrm{tmul}}\)/\(*\_{\mathrm{apply}}\)
522	  equations are pure-\(\mathrm{rfl}\) computation rules on elementary tensors and
523	  are private implementation glue.
524	\end{proof}
525	
526	\begin{lemma}[Relative tensor of module presheaves as a coequalizer]
527	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
528	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relativeTensorCoequalizerIso}
529	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
530	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
531	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
532	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
533	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
534	  underlying abelian-group presheaf of their relative tensor product
535	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
536	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is naturally the coequalizer of the
537	  parallel pair
538	  \[
539	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
540	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
541	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
542	  \]
543	  of abelian-group presheaves, where the first map acts the ring factor on the left,
544	  \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto (r \cdot p) \otimes q\), and the second on the
545	  right, \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto p \otimes (r \cdot q)\). The identification
546	  is objectwise and natural in \(U\).
547	\end{lemma}
548	
549	\begin{proof}
550	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
551	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
552	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
553	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
554	  The argument promotes the objectwise coequalizer of
555	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} to the functor category, in
556	  three steps.
557	
558	  \textbf{1. Objectwise.} For each open \(U\), apply
559	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} with \(S = \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
560	  \(M = \mathcal{P}(U)\), \(N = \mathcal{Q}(U)\): the abelian group
561	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is the coequalizer
562	  in \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps
563	  \[
564	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
565	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
566	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
567	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
568	  \]
569	  with cofork map the projection \(\pi_U\).
570	
571	  \textbf{2. Naturality / promotion.} The three objectwise families assemble into
572	  morphisms of abelian-group presheaves
573	  \[
574	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
575	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
576	    \;\overset{a_L,\,a_R}{\rightrightarrows}\;
577	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
578	    \;\overset{\pi}{\longrightarrow}\;
579	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr),
580	  \]
581	  i.e.\ natural transformations of functors
582	  \(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{AddCommGrp}\). Naturality of
583	  \(a_L, a_R, \pi\) in \(U\) is the compatibility of the module action with the
584	  restriction maps of \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{Q}\): the restriction
585	  of \(s \cdot m\) is \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), and likewise for the projection,
586	  because the relative tensor is functorial in \(U\). Colimits in a functor
587	  category are computed objectwise --- by
588	  \cref{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib} a cocone is a colimit as
589	  soon as every evaluation \((\mathrm{ev}_U)\) of it is --- so the objectwise
590	  coequalizers of step 1 assemble into a coequalizer of the displayed parallel
591	  pair in \(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{AddCommGrp}\).
592	
593	  \textbf{3. Identification of the apex.} The apex presheaf
594	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is, by the
595	  objectwise tensor formula of the presheaf monoidal structure
596	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}: \((\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes_p
597	  \mathcal{M}_2)(U) = \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
598	  \mathcal{M}_2(U)\)), exactly the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the
599	  relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
600	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Transporting the colimit of step 2
601	  along this identification exhibits
602	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\), as an abelian-group
603	  presheaf, as the coequalizer of the parallel pair, naturally in \(U\); this is
604	  the isomorphism \texttt{relativeTensorCoequalizerIso}.
605	
606	  \textbf{Caveat to verify in formalisation.} The \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf
607	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}\) appearing in the rows is the
608	  abelian (objectwise \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor) presheaf, whose value on \(U\) is
609	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). This is the monoidal
610	  product placed on abelian-group presheaves by the construction underlying
611	  \(\mathrm{GrothendieckTopology.W.monoidal}\) (\texttt{Sites/Monoidal.lean}); one
612	  must check that \emph{this} is the structure used, so that the same rows are the
613	  ones \(\mathrm{W}\) inverts in
614	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}.
615	\end{proof}
616	
617	\begin{lemma}[Objectwise colimits in a functor category]
618	  \label{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib}
619	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Limits.evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits}
620	  \mathlibok
621	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
622	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.FunctorCategory.Basic}).}
623	  Let \(\mathcal{D}\) be a category and \(\mathcal{A}\) a category in which the
624	  relevant colimits exist. A cocone over a diagram
625	  \(F : \mathcal{J} \to (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A})\) in the functor category is
626	  a colimit cocone as soon as, for every object \(U\) of \(\mathcal{D}\), its image
627	  under evaluation \(\mathrm{ev}_U : (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A}) \to \mathcal{A}\)
628	  is a colimit cocone in \(\mathcal{A}\). Equivalently, colimits in a functor
629	  category are computed objectwise.
630	\end{lemma}

632	\begin{lemma}[Objectwise value of the presheaf tensor product]
633	  \label{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
634	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj_obj}
635	  \mathlibok
636	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
637	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
638	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
639	  commutative rings and \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\) presheaves of modules over
640	  \(R\). For every object \(U\) the value of their monoidal product

646	  \]

650	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
651	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
652	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
653	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
654	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
655	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
656	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
657	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
658	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
659	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
660	  the sheafification adjunction
661	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
662	  presheaves of modules, and write
663	  \[
664	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
665	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
666	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
667	  \]
668	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
669	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
670	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
671	  \[
672	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
673	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
674	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
675	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
676	  \]
677	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
678	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
679	\end{lemma}
680	
681	\begin{proof}
682	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
683	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
684	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
685	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
686	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
687	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
688	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
689	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
690	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
691	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
692	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
693	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
694	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
695	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
696	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
697	
698	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
699	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
700	  is the objectwise relative tensor
701	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
702	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
703	  maps
704	  \[
705	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
706	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
707	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
708	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
709	  \]
710	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
711	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
712	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
713	  pair
714	  \[
715	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
716	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
717	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
718	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
719	  \]
720	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
721	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
722	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
723	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
724	
725	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
726	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
727	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
728	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
729	  of \(a\)-images. Now the localization unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}}\) lies in
730	  \(J.W\), and the class \(J.W\) is \emph{monoidal} for the
731	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor: whiskering a local isomorphism by
732	  \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\) again yields a local isomorphism. (This is the
733	  abelian monoidality of \(J.W\), itself proved through the fact that the
734	  internal hom into a sheaf is again a sheaf.) Consequently the two whiskered
735	  comparison maps
736	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) and
737	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
738	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) relating the two rows are
739	  isomorphisms.
740	
741	  An isomorphism between two parallel-pair diagrams induces an isomorphism of
742	  their coequalizers; applied to the rows above, this shows that \(a\) sends the
743	  underlying abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) to an
744	  isomorphism. Hence that morphism lies in \(J.W\), so
745	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
746	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
747	\end{proof}
748	
749	\begin{corollary}[Sheaf-level associator on tensor products]
750	  \label{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}
751	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjAssoc}
752	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
753	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
754	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
755	  For sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B},
756	  \mathcal{C}\) there is a canonical associativity isomorphism
757	  \[
758	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}} :
759	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{B})
760	      \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C}
761	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
762	    \mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
763	      (\mathcal{B} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C})
764	  \]
765	  of sheaves of modules (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), natural in all three
766	  arguments.
767	\end{corollary}
768	
769	\begin{proof}
770	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
771	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
772	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
773	  Write \(a, b, c\) for the underlying presheaves of modules of
774	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) and \((-)^{\#}\) for sheafification
775	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}). By
776	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} the two iterated products unfold to
777	  \[
778	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
779	      = \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#},
780	    \qquad
781	    \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
782	      = \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#},
783	  \]
784	  each carrying an \emph{inner} sheafification that obstructs a direct appeal to
785	  the presheaf associator. The whiskered units remove these inner
786	  sheafifications. Applying \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
787	  with \(\mathcal{P} = a \otimes_p b\) and \(\mathcal{Q} = c\), the morphism
788	  \[
789	    (\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#} :
790	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
791	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
792	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
793	    = (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
794	  \]
795	  is an isomorphism; and, reading the same lemma through the braiding of the
796	  presheaf monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) so as
797	  to whisker the unit \(\eta_{b \otimes_p c}\) on the left of \(a\), the
798	  morphism
799	  \[
800	    (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#} :
801	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)\bigr)^{\#}
802	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
803	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#}
804	    = \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
805	  \]
806	  is an isomorphism. Between the two un-sheafified middle terms sits the
807	  sheafification of the presheaf associator
808	  \[
809	    \alpha^p_{a,b,c} :
810	    (a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c
811	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
812	    a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)
813	  \]
814	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), an isomorphism because
815	  sheafification is a functor and \(\alpha^p\) is one. The associator is the
816	  composite
817	  \[
818	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}}
819	    = (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#}
820	      \circ (\alpha^p_{a,b,c})^{\#}
821	      \circ \bigl((\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#}\bigr)^{-1},
822	  \]
823	  an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
824	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) follows from naturality of the unit,
825	  of the whiskerings, and of the presheaf associator.
826	\end{proof}
827	
828	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
829	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
830	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
831	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
832	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
833	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
834	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
835	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
836	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
837	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
838	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
839	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
840	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
841	  %  (formulation omitted)."
842	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
843	  power isomorphisms).}
844	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
845	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
846	  \[
847	    \mu_{m,m'} :
848	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
849	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
850	  \]
851	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
852	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
853	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
854	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
855	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
856	\end{lemma}
857	
858	\begin{proof}
859	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
860	    def:tensorObjUnitIso, def:tensorBraiding,
861	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
862	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
863	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
864	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
865	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
866	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
867	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
868	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
869	
870	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
871	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
872	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
873	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
874	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
875	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
876	  sheaf tensor products
877	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
878	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
879	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
880	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
881	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
882	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
883	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
884	  of the tensor product is never used.
885	
886	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
887	  \(m = 0\),
888	  \[
889	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
890	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
891	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
892	  \]
893	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
894	  recursive definition
895	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
896	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
897	  \[
898	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
899	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
900	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
901	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
902	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
903	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
904	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
905	  \]
906	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
907	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
908	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
909	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
910	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
911	  \[
912	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
913	      \mathcal{L}
914	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
915	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
916	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
917	  \]
918	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
919	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
920	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
921	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
922	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
923	
924	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
925	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
926	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
927	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
928	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
929	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
930	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
931	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
932	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
933	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
934	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
935	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
936	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
937	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
938	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
939	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
940	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
941	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
942	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
943	\end{proof}

945	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
946	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
947	
948	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
949	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
950	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
951	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
952	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
953	global sections.
954	
955	\begin{definition}\leanok
956	[Section multiplication]
957	  \label{def:sectionMul}
958	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
959	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
960	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
961	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
962	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
963	  \[
964	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
965	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
966	    \;\longrightarrow\;
967	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
968	  \]
969	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
970	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
971	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
972	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
973	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
974	  the sheafification unit
975	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
976	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
977	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
978	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
979	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
980	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
981	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
982	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
983	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
984	\end{definition}
985	
986	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
987	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
988	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
989	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
990	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
991	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
992	  the two composites
993	  \[
994	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
995	    \longrightarrow
996	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
997	    \cong
998	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
999	  \]
1000	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
1001	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
1002	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
1003	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
1004	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
1005	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
1006	\end{lemma}
1007	
1008	\begin{proof}
1009	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1010	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1011	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
1012	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
1013	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
1014	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
1015	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
1016	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
1017	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
1018	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
1019	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
1020	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
1021	  section multiplications.
1022	\end{proof}
1023	
1024	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
1025	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
1026	
1027	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
1028	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
1029	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
1030	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
1031	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
1032	
1033	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
1034	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1035	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
1036	  \mathlibok
1037	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
1038	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1039	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
1040	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
1041	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
1042	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
1043	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
1044	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
1045	  \(1 \in A_0\).
1046	\end{lemma}
1047	
1048	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
1049	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
1050	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
1051	  \mathlibok
1052	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
1053	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
1054	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
1055	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1056	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
1057	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
1058	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
1059	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
1060	  extension of the graded action.
1061	\end{lemma}
1062	
1063	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
1064	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1065	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1066	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
1067	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
1068	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
1069	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
1070	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
1071	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
1072	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1073	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
1074	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
1075	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
1076	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
1077	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
1078	  %  $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
1079	  %  We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
1080	  %  and associative ring with $1$."
1081	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
1082	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
1083	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
1084	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules

\textbf{3. Identification of the apex.} The apex presheaf
  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is, by the
  objectwise tensor formula of the presheaf monoidal structure
  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}: \((\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes_p
  \mathcal{M}_2)(U) = \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
  \mathcal{M}_2(U)\)), exactly the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the
  relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Transporting the colimit of step 2
  along this identification exhibits
  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\), as an abelian-group
  presheaf, as the coequalizer of the parallel pair, naturally in \(U\); this is
  the isomorphism \texttt{relativeTensorCoequalizerIso}.

  \textbf{Caveat to verify in formalisation.} The \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf
  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}\) appearing in the rows is the
  abelian (objectwise \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor) presheaf, whose value on \(U\) is
  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). This is the monoidal
  product placed on abelian-group presheaves by the construction underlying
  \(\mathrm{GrothendieckTopology.W.monoidal}\) (\texttt{Sites/Monoidal.lean}); one
  must check that \emph{this} is the structure used, so that the same rows are the
  ones \(\mathrm{W}\) inverts in
  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}.
\end{proof}

# Blueprint-clean report — iter-054

## Status: DONE

All three edited chapters cleaned. No new SOURCE quotes needed (all new blocks are
Archon-original / standard-fact restatements). No `\mathlibok` removed. No `\leanok`
touched. All LaTeX environments balanced.

---

## Changes made

### `Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`

**G3 proof (`lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly`):**
- Removed informal citation references `\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.9, flat families)` from the opening sentence of the proof.
  Reason: all four new B2 blocks (`gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq`, `gf_section_localization_twoleg`, `gf_base_localization_comparison`, `gf_crossChart_spanning_cover`) and the G3 assembly are Archon-original; no citation belongs in the proof under the surrounding unsourced-block convention.

---

### `Picard_GrassmannianQuot.tex`

**`lem:gr_opensMap_final` proof:**
- Removed trailing sentence "This is the general ``pullback of free is free'' unlock, reusable beyond this chapter." — project-specific meta-commentary not appropriate in a timeless mathematical document.

**Connecting prose between `lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp` and `def:grassmannian_functor`:**
- Replaced `(\(\mathrm{map\_id}\) and \(\mathrm{map\_comp}\))` with `(identity and composition)` — `map_id`/`map_comp` are Lean functor-field names, not mathematical terminology.

All six new blocks (`gr_opensMap_final`, `gr_pullbackFreeIso`, `gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank`, `def:gr_rankQuotient`, `gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id`, `gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp`) have correct `\label`, `\lean{}`, `\uses{}` (where applicable). No source quotes needed.

---

### `Picard_SectionGradedRing.tex`

**`lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer` proof:**

Three Mathlib anchors (`lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib`, `lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib`, `lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib`) retain their `\mathlibok` markers and are unchanged.
